title: Moving a hive under three miles
subtitle: How to relocate bees without losing the foragers.
excerpt: Moving a hive across the garden or to a neighbouring field sits in the most awkward range for bees: far enough that the old site is still visible, close enough that foragers return to where the hive used to be. The standard approach is to either move at least three miles or use the 'two metre then three miles' method.
sourceNotes: National Bee Unit Beekeeper's Toolkit, nationalbeeunit.com. BBKA technical guidance on moving bees. Hooper, T., Guide to Bees and Honey (1976).

glossaryTerms.definition: An older worker bee that has memorised the position of the hive and navigates back to that fixed location on every return flight. Foragers returning to an empty stand will cluster there, waiting.

Short circling flights taken by young bees as they fix the position of the hive in memory. Moving the hive before orientation flights are locked in is easier than moving foragers with established home positions.

content.text: The three-mile rule exists because bees navigate by visual landmarks within a radius of about three miles from the hive. A

moved two hundred metres from its old stand will fly back to where the hive used to be, wait, and eventually die there. Move the same bee thirty miles and she is in entirely unfamiliar territory; she reorients and accepts the new stand as home.

When you need to move a hive within a short distance, across the garden, to a different part of the holding, you have two options. Move two metres at a time over many weeks, or move more than three miles, keep the hive there for three to four weeks, then bring it back to the new position.
Move the hive two metres per day in the direction of its new position. Bees extend their
daily, so a gradual shift falls within their adaptive range. At two metres a day, a twenty-metre relocation takes ten days.

Move the hive before the first foragers leave in the morning, ideally before 7 a.m. on a still day. Each move should happen on consecutive days, not with gaps; a week's pause between moves resets the foragers' home position.

Move the hive to a temporary site at least three miles away for three to four weeks. After that time the forager population has turned over and the new bees have learned the temporary site as home. Move the hive back to its new permanent position. The bees are now in unfamiliar territory again and reorient to the new stand.

This is the preferred method when the final destination is more than ten metres from the current site. It's more disruptive in the short term but creates fewer lost foragers than a slow daily shuffle.
Close the entrance the evening before the move, after all foragers have returned. Use foam or a mesh entrance block secured with a strap. Smoke the hive lightly before closing to settle the bees.

Strap the brood box and any supers together before lifting. A hive in a car boot on a warm day will overheat inside thirty minutes; if moving by vehicle, ventilate the mesh floor and keep the journey short. Never close a hive in a sealed boot in summer.

You need to notify the National Bee Unit on BeeBase if you move bees to a new address, not just a new position within the same holding. Registration is free and keeps you on the local disease-alert network.

Open the entrance as soon as the hive is on its new stand. The bees will take orientation flights immediately. On the two-metre method, a few foragers will drift back to the old position the first day; by the third day the drift should have stopped.

Put something unfamiliar in front of the entrance for the first twenty-four hours after arrival. A leafy branch propped against the hive works well, or a piece of sacking hung over the front. When bees look closely at their entrance on the way out, they take a fresh orientation flight rather than flying back to the old position.
